snp3.11:1.4:
Asita the seer, in his mid-day meditation, saw the Group of Thirty— Sakka the king,
and devas dressed in pure white exultant, ecstatic— holding up banners, cheering wildly,
snp3.11:2.1: & on seeing the devas so joyful & happy,
snp3.11:2.2: having paid his respects, he said:
snp3.11:2.3: “Why is the deva community so wildly elated?
snp3.11:2.4: Why are they holding up banners & waving them around?
snp3.11:3.1: Even after the war with the Asuras
snp3.11:3.2: —when victory was the devas’, the Asuras defeated—
snp3.11:3.3: even then there was nothing hair-raising like this.
snp3.11:3.4: Seeing what marvel are the devas so joyful?
snp3.11:4.1: They whistle, they sing, play music,
snp3.11:4.3: So I ask you, who live on Mount Meru’s summit.
snp3.11:4.4: Please dispel my doubt quickly, dear sirs.”
snp3.11:5.2: has been born for welfare & happiness in the human world,
snp3.11:5.3: in a town in the Sakyan countryside, Lumbini.
snp3.11:5.4: That’s why we’re contented, so wildly elated.
snp3.11:6.1: He, the highest of all beings, the ultimate person,
snp3.11:6.2: a bull among men, highest of all people,
snp3.11:6.3: will set turning the Wheel [of Dhamma] in the forest named after the seers,
snp3.11:6.4: like a strong, roaring lion, the conqueror of beasts.”
snp3.11:7.1: Hearing these words, Asita quickly descended [from heaven]
snp3.11:7.3: There, taking a seat, he said to the Sakyans:
snp3.11:7.4:
“Where is the prince?
I, too, want to see him.”
snp3.11:8.4:
The Sakyans then showed to the seer named Asita their son, the prince, like gold aglow,
burnished by a most skillful smith in the mouth of the furnace, blazing with glory, flawless in color.
snp3.11:9.1: On seeing the prince blazing like flame,
snp3.11:9.2: pure like the bull of the stars going across the sky
snp3.11:9.3: —the burning sun, released from the clouds of autumn—
snp3.11:9.4: he was exultant, filled with abundant rapture.
snp3.11:10.4:
The devas held in the sky a many-spoked sunshade of a thousand circles.
Gold-handled whisks waved up & down, but those holding the whisks & the sunshade couldn’t be seen.
snp3.11:11.1: The coiled-haired seer named Dark Splendor,
snp3.11:11.2: seeing the boy, like an ornament of gold on the red woolen blanket,
snp3.11:11.3: a white sunshade held over his head,
snp3.11:11.4: received him, joyful in mind & pleased.
snp3.11:12.1: And on receiving the bull of the Sakyans,
snp3.11:12.2: longingly, the master of mantras & signs
snp3.11:12.4: “This one is unsurpassed, the highest of the biped race.”
snp3.11:13.1: Then, foreseeing his own imminent departure,
snp3.11:13.3: On seeing him weeping, the Sakyans asked:
snp3.11:13.4: “But surely there will be no danger for the prince?”
snp3.11:14.1: On seeing the Sakyans’ concern he replied,
snp3.11:14.2: “I foresee for the prince no harm.
snp3.11:14.3: Nor will there be any danger for him.
snp3.11:14.4:
This one’s not insignificant:
Be assured.
snp3.11:15.1: This prince will touch the ultimate self-awakening.
snp3.11:15.3: will set rolling the Wheel of Dhamma through sympathy for the welfare of many.
snp3.11:15.4: His holy life will spread far & wide.
snp3.11:16.1: But as for me, my life here has no long remainder.
snp3.11:16.2: My death will take place before then.
snp3.11:16.3: I won’t get to hear the Dhamma of this one with the peerless role.
snp3.11:16.4: That’s why I’m stricken, afflicted, & pained.”
snp3.11:17.1: He, having brought the Sakyans abundant rapture,
snp3.11:17.2: the follower of the holy life left the inner chamber and,
snp3.11:17.3: out of sympathy for his nephew,
snp3.11:17.4: urged him on toward the Dhamma of the one with the peerless role:
snp3.11:18.1:
“When you hear from another the word,
‘Awakened One,’
snp3.11:18.2: or ‘Attaining self-awakening, he lays open the path of the Dhamma,’
snp3.11:18.3: go there and, asking him yourself,
snp3.11:18.4: follow the holy life under that Blessed One.”
snp3.11:19.1: Instructed by the one whose mind was set on his benefit, Such,
snp3.11:19.2: seeing in the future the utmost purity,
snp3.11:19.3: Nālaka, who had laid up a store of merit,
snp3.11:19.4: awaited the Victor expectantly, guarding his senses.
snp3.11:20.1: On hearing word of the Victor’s turning of the foremost wheel,
snp3.11:20.2:
he went, he saw the bull among seers.
Confident,
snp3.11:20.3: he asked the foremost sage about the utmost sagacity,
snp3.11:20.4:
now that Asita’s forecast had come to pass.
Nālaka:
snp3.11:21.4: you who have gone to the beyond of all dhammas.
snp3.11:22.1: I’m intent on the homeless life;
snp3.11:22.4:
the highest state of sagacity.”
The Buddha:
snp3.11:23.3: a sagacity hard to do, hard to master.
snp3.11:24.2: for in a village there’s praise & abuse.
snp3.11:24.3: Ward off any flaw in the heart.
snp3.11:25.1: High & low things will come up
snp3.11:27.2: As are these, so am I.’
snp3.11:27.4: neither kill nor get others to kill.
snp3.11:29.1: Stomach not full, moderate in food,
snp3.11:29.4: One without hunger is one who’s unbound.
snp3.11:30.1: Having gone on his almsround, the sage
snp3.11:30.2: should then go to the forest,
snp3.11:30.3: approaching the root of a tree,
snp3.11:31.1: The enlightened one, intent on jhāna,
snp3.11:31.2: should find delight in the forest,
snp3.11:31.3: should practice jhāna at the foot of a tree,
snp3.11:32.1: Then, at the end of the night,
snp3.11:32.2: he should go to the village,
snp3.11:33.2: the sage should not go forcing his way among families.
snp3.11:33.4: he shouldn’t utter a scheming word.
snp3.11:34.2:
I got nothing.
That, too, is good.’
snp3.11:34.3: Being such with regard to both,
snp3.11:34.4: he returns to the very same tree.
snp3.11:35.1: Wandering with his bowl in hand
snp3.11:35.3: he shouldn’t despise a piddling gift
snp3.11:36.3: They don’t go twice to the further shore.
snp3.11:37.1: In one who has no attachment—
snp3.11:37.2: the monk who has cut the stream,
snp3.11:37.3: abandoning what is & isn’t a duty—
snp3.11:39.3: Be free of raw stench, independent,
snp3.11:39.4: having the holy life as your aim.
snp3.11:41.1: On hearing the fame of the enlightened
snp3.11:41.4: my disciple should foster all the more shame & conviction.
snp3.11:42.3: Those in small channels flow noisily,
snp3.11:43.3: The fool is like a half-empty pot;
snp3.11:43.4: one who is wise, a full lake.
snp3.11:44.1: A contemplative who speaks a great deal
snp3.11:44.4: knowing, he speaks a great deal.
snp3.11:45.1: But he who, knowing, is restrained,
snp3.11:45.2: knowing, doesn’t speak a great deal:
snp3.11:45.3: He is a sage worthy of sagehood.
snp3.11:45.4: He is a sage, his sagehood attained.”
